name: Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP)
description: Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP) — highest airway pressure during a delivered breath, in cmH2O.

en.name: Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP)
en.shortDescription: Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP) — highest airway pressure during a delivered breath, in cmH2O.
en.longDescription:
## Definition & Clinical Context

Peak inspiratory pressure (PIP) is the highest airway pressure reached during the delivery of a breath by the ventilator. It reflects the combined pressure needed to overcome airway resistance and lung/chest-wall elastance; a rising PIP signals increased resistance (e.g. secretions, bronchospasm) or decreased compliance. In source data it is a single numeric value per patient-timestamp, measured by the ventilator. It is recorded in intensive care and anaesthesia for ventilated patients. It is reported in centimetres of water (cmH2O); some ventilators display mbar (1 cmH2O ≈ 0.98 mbar).

## Included Concepts

- *4101694 / 27913002 — Peak inspiratory pressure (SNOMED)*: the maximum airway pressure during a delivered breath.

**Note on units**: reported in **cmH2O** (`44777590 / cm[H2O] — centimeter watercolumn`, UCUM), the recommended unit, with a registered cmH2O↔mbar conversion (1 cmH2O = 0.98 mbar).

## Mapping Notes

- Map a recorded peak inspiratory pressure (PIP, Ppeak) to *4101694 / 27913002 — Peak inspiratory pressure*. Convert mbar to cmH2O (×1.02) before mapping.
- **Not plateau, mean or PEEP**: plateau pressure (during an inspiratory hold), mean airway pressure and PEEP are distinct pressures in their own concept sets.

fr.shortDescription: Pression inspiratoire de pointe (PIP) — pression maximale des voies aériennes lors d’un cycle délivré, en cmH2O.
